column1: # Principle: Nested forward calls require parens; bare function words
# in arg position are structural boundaries.
# A natural-looking form like `addq 1 mulq 2 3` (read as
# `addq(1, mulq(2, 3))`) does NOT work in aqleng. The matchSignature
# loop (aqleng/go/match.go) explicitly treats a registered function
# word inside the forward scan as a structural BOUNDARY:
#     // 1.4: function word — boundary, stop.
#     if e.registry.Lookup(ww.Name) != nil {
#         break
# So when `addq` is collecting forward args and meets `mulq`, the
# scan stops with only one slot filled. Since the stack is empty,
# no overload satisfies → signature_error.
# To nest, the inner call must be PRE-EVALUATED via parens. The
# engine's preEvalParens pass runs over the forward window before
# matchSignature so the matcher sees the paren's residual as a
# concrete value (or values) on the future stack.
# Rules being asserted
# 1. Bare `outer inner …` chains where `inner` is a registered word
#    in the outer's arg slot ALWAYS error with signature_error.
#    (Both sides forward-prec, both 2-arg, regardless of order.)
# 2. Wrapping the inner in parens fixes the chain — paren bodies
#    pre-evaluate to a concrete value that fills the outer slot.
# 3. Left-to-right intermediate-on-stack works WITHOUT parens — the
#    inner fires first because its tokens come left of the outer.
# 4. A def whose body is a list (`def NAME [body]`) splices its body
#    AT THE PUSH SITE — there is no "wait for forward args" mode for
#    list-body defs. So `def doub [dupq addq] doub 5` fails: the body
#    is spliced immediately and `dupq` runs on an empty stack.
#    `5 doub` works because 5 is pushed first, then `doub` splices
#    its body which finds 5.
# 5. A def whose body is an `fn`-built FnDefInfo IS forward-collecting
#    via the synthesised native handler, so `double 5` works after
#    `def double (fn [...])`.
# 6. Even forward-collecting fn-defs cannot serve as inner words in
#    another forward-collecting outer — the outer's matchSignature
#    still hits the registered-word boundary.
# Why this matters
# The boundary rule trades expressive power for unambiguity: a reader
# can scan left-to-right and know that each forward-collecting word
# absorbs ONLY non-word literals (and pre-evaluated paren groups).
# Without it, `addq 1 mulq 2 3` would be ambiguous between
# `addq(1, mulq(2, 3))` and "the 5-token program that left a residue".
# The engine picks the unambiguous reading: parens are explicit, the
# rest is a flat scan.
# This file is the regression test for the boundary rule. Each
# "should error" row pins the rule down; each "with parens" row
# shows the workaround the language design intends.
# tokens
column3: note

column1: # 1. Bare function-in-arg-slot ALWAYS errors.
addq 1 mulq 2 3
column3: addq scans forward, hits mulq (registered), breaks at fwd=1; stack empty; no match

column1: addq mulq 2 3 1
column3: mulq is at sig[0]; boundary at first scan step

column1: mulq addq 1 2 3
column3: mulq scans forward, hits addq boundary; no match

column1: subq mulq 2 3 1
column3: subq also boundaries on mulq

column1: 1 addq mulq 2 3
column3: prefix 1 + bare-inner-forward still errors

column1: # 2. Wrapping the inner in parens fixes the chain.
addq 1 ( mulq 2 3 )
column3: paren pre-evaluates to 6 → addq(1,6) = 7

column1: addq ( mulq 2 3 ) 1
column3: paren in sig[0]; sig[1] forward = 1 → addq(6,1) = 7

column1: mulq ( addq 1 2 ) 3
column3: paren=3 fills mulq sig[0]; sig[1]=3 → mulq(3,3)=9

column1: subq ( mulq 2 3 ) 1
column3: paren = 6 (mulq commutative); fwd=[6, 1] → sig=[6, 1] → handler returns 1-6 = -5

column1: mulq ( subq 10 3 ) 2
column3: paren = 3-10 = -7 (subq: fwd=[10, 3] → sig=[10, 3] → b-a = -7); outer mulq fwd=[-7, 2] → -14

column1: addq 1 ( mulq 2 ( addq 3 4 ) )
column3: deep nesting via parens: addq 3 4 = 7; mulq 2 7 = 14; addq 1 14 = 15

column1: # 3. Left-to-right intermediate-on-stack works without parens.
addq 2 3 mulq 4
column3: addq fires first → stack=[5]; mulq collects forward 4 with stack 5 → 5*4=20

column1: 2 3 addq mulq 4 5
column3: addq fires producing 5 on stack; mulq collects 4,5 ALL FORWARD; mulq=20; the 5 from addq stays as residue. Note the surprise: mulq does NOT consume the stack-resident 5 because its forward scan succeeds first.

column1: # 4. List-body defs splice immediately — no forward-collection.
def doub [ dupq addq ] doub 5
column3: doub splices [dupq addq] at pointer; dupq needs 1 stack value, sees empty stack

column1: def doub [ dupq addq ] 5 doub
column3: 5 pushed first, then doub splices; dupq→[5,5], addq→[10]

column1: # 5. fn-built defs DO forward-collect (synthesised native handler).
def double ( fn [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ) double 5
column3: fn-def is a forward-prec native; collects 5 from forward

column1: def double ( fn [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ) 5 double
column3: prefix form also works

column1: # 6. Even fn-built defs can't serve as inner forward in a bare chain.
def double ( fn [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ) addq 1 double 5
column3: addq still boundaries on the registered double word

column1: def double ( fn [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ) addq 1 ( double 5 )
column3: paren pre-evaluates double(5)=10 into addq's sig[1] slot; addq(1,10)=11
